APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses semantic parsing and natural language interfaces by extending a core programming language via interactive user definitions, implemented on SEMPRE with online AdaGrad updates. The methodology (grammar induction, alignment-based chart extension) and evaluation on the Voxelurn user-community (240 complex structures; 74.7% naturalized utterances) follow standards expected for the community. The focus on learning from interactive definitions and scaling to large action spaces is directly relevant to NLP/ML researchers working on language grounding and interactive learning. | B(5): This paper addresses natural language interfaces and interactive semantic parsing—core topics for top-tier NLP/ML venues—by seeding a core programming language and learning from user definitions, implemented on SEMPRE with online AdaGrad. The methodology (grammar induction from user head/body definitions, conservative alignment-based chart extension) and evaluation (community-built Voxelurn corpus with 240 structures and 74.7% naturalized utterances in the last 10K) match the empirical and methodological standards expected at such conferences. The comparison to prior interactive weak denotational supervision work and the focus on scaling to large action spaces make the work well positioned for the NLP/ML research community.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses semantic parsing and natural language interfaces by extending a core programming language via interactive user definitions, implemented on SEMPRE with online AdaGrad updates. The methodology (grammar induction, alignment-based chart extension) and evaluation on the Voxelurn user-community (240 complex structures; 74.7% naturalized utterances) follow standards expected for the community. The focus on learning from interactive definitions and scaling to large action spaces is directly relevant to NLP/ML researchers working on language grounding and interactive learning.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses natural language interfaces and interactive semantic parsing—core topics for top-tier NLP/ML venues—by seeding a core programming language and learning from user definitions, implemented on SEMPRE with online AdaGrad. The methodology (grammar induction from user head/body definitions, conservative alignment-based chart extension) and evaluation (community-built Voxelurn corpus with 240 structures and 74.7% naturalized utterances in the last 10K) match the empirical and methodological standards expected at such conferences. The comparison to prior interactive weak denotational supervision work and the focus on scaling to large action spaces make the work well positioned for the NLP/ML research community.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable new approach by seeding a core programming language and letting users incrementally "naturalize" it via head/body definitions, which is a distinct perspective compared to prior selection-from-candidate supervision. The use of alignment-based transplantation of derivations into the parse chart to induce paraphrase-generalizing rules (with conservative heuristics) is a non-trivial technical extension beyond existing interactive semantic-parsing work. The application to a large action space (Voxelurn) and empirical evidence of community-driven growth (240 structures; 74.7% naturalized utterances) further differentiate it from prior methods that cannot scale by candidate selection alone. | B(4): The paper proposes a distinct interactive approach—seeding a core programming language and letting users define naturalized constructs (head->body) and inducing grammar rules—which differs from standard supervised or selection-from-candidate paradigms. Its alignment-based transplant of derivations into the chart to infer paraphrases (e.g., 'up' -> 'top') is a concrete, non-trivial technical extension to improve generalization while maintaining precision. The work specifically targets scalability to large action spaces (Voxelurn) that prior weak denotational supervision methods struggle with, and demonstrates community-driven language growth (240 structures; 74.7% naturalized utterances), supporting that the contribution is a notable novel advance rather than purely incremental.

The application to a large action space (Voxelurn) and empirical evidence of community-driven growth (240 structures; 74.7% naturalized utterances) further differentiate it from prior methods that cannot scale by candidate selection alone.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments provide real-user evidence (240 complex structures; naturalized language reached 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and compare to prior weak supervision work, supporting the core claims. However, important analyses are missing or limited: there are no reported hyperparameters or code for reproduction, ablation/sensitivity studies are not described (alignment heuristics and conservative application are asserted but not systematically evaluated), and generalization limits are partly acknowledged but not thoroughly quantified. Related work coverage is adequate and several limitations are discussed, but the lack of reproducibility details and deeper ablations prevents a higher score. | B(3): The experiments demonstrate the core claims on the Voxelurn corpus (users built 240 complex voxel structures; naturalized language rose to 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and the paper compares to an interactive weak-supervision baseline. The authors discuss specific limitations (e.g., selection-from-candidate infeasible for large action spaces; initial rule induction undergeneralizes; alignment extension applied conservatively) and related work coverage is adequate. However, the work appears to lack ablation/sensitivity studies quantifying the benefit of alignment-based rule induction, and key reproducibility details are missing (code not mentioned, hyperparameters not reported), leaving some important analyses unexamined.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments provide real-user evidence (240 complex structures; naturalized language reached 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and compare to prior weak supervision work, supporting the core claims. However, important analyses are missing or limited: there are no reported hyperparameters or code for reproduction, ablation/sensitivity studies are not described (alignment heuristics and conservative application are asserted but not systematically evaluated), and generalization limits are partly acknowledged but not thoroughly quantified. Related work coverage is adequate and several limitations are discussed, but the lack of reproducibility details and deeper ablations prevents a higher score.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments demonstrate the core claims on the Voxelurn corpus (users built 240 complex voxel structures; naturalized language rose to 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and the paper compares to an interactive weak-supervision baseline. The authors discuss specific limitations (e.g., selection-from-candidate infeasible for large action spaces; initial rule induction undergeneralizes; alignment extension applied conservatively) and related work coverage is adequate. However, the work appears to lack ablation/sensitivity studies quantifying the benefit of alignment-based rule induction, and key reproducibility details are missing (code not mentioned, hyperparameters not reported), leaving some important analyses unexamined.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are presented early (seeded core language, user-defined head/body definitions, and alignment-based chart extension) with a clear description of methodology and experiments on the Voxelurn dataset. Figures/examples and experimental metrics (240 structures built; 74.7% naturalized utterances) make the results concrete, and limitations are explicitly discussed (e.g., infeasibility of selection-from-candidate supervision, conservative alignment heuristics). Presentation weaknesses are minor: reproducibility details are missing (code not mentioned, hyperparameters not reported), and some methodological choices (when chart extension is applied) are described as constrained, suggesting places where clearer justification or more detail would help readers. | B(4): Overall the paper appears well organized and the main contributions are stated early (seeding a core language, interactive definitions, and alignment-based chart extension). Notation and method presentation seem consistent and intelligible (head/body definitions, SEMPRE implementation, online AdaGrad) and related work coverage is adequate. Minor presentation gaps are noted in the summary: writing is described as only "adequate," and important details for clarity/reproducibility (e.g., hyperparameters, code availability, and no mention of figures/tables) are not reported, which likely reflects small presentation weaknesses rather than major clarity problems.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents concrete experimental evidence (240 user-built voxel structures; naturalized language accounting for 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and describes an implementation on SEMPRE with online AdaGrad, which supports the main interactive-learning claims. However, key experimental details are missing (no hyperparameters, no code release, no reported statistical tests) and the claim that selection-from-candidate supervision is infeasible at scale is asserted rather than empirically quantified against alternatives. The use of conservative alignment heuristics is plausible and noted as a limitation, but the restricted applicability (only single-utterance bodies, few unmatched tokens) and lack of detailed baseline comparisons leave some methodological concerns unresolved. | B(4): The main claims are reasonably supported by empirical evidence in the summary (users built 240 complex voxel structures; naturalized language accounted for 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and the method is compared to a relevant baseline (interactive weak denotational supervision). However, no mathematical derivations or proofs are reported, and experimental reporting is incomplete (no hyperparameters, code availability, or statistical tests mentioned), which weakens reproducibility and rigorous evaluation. The paper acknowledges conservative, limited alignment heuristics (only applied for single-utterance bodies), indicating a possible undergeneralization risk but not a fatal flaw. Overall the technical approach appears sound with minor methodological/reporting gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents concrete experimental evidence (240 user-built voxel structures; naturalized language accounting for 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and describes an implementation on SEMPRE with online AdaGrad, which supports the main interactive-learning claims. The use of conservative alignment heuristics is plausible and noted as a limitation, but the restricted applicability (only single-utterance bodies, few unmatched tokens) and lack of detailed baseline comparisons leave some methodological concerns unresolved.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are reasonably supported by empirical evidence in the summary (users built 240 complex voxel structures; naturalized language accounted for 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) and the method is compared to a relevant baseline (interactive weak denotational supervision). However, no mathematical derivations or proofs are reported, and experimental reporting is incomplete (no hyperparameters, code availability, or statistical tests mentioned), which weakens reproducibility and rigorous evaluation. The paper acknowledges conservative, limited alignment heuristics (only applied for single-utterance bodies), indicating a possible undergeneralization risk but not a fatal flaw. Overall the technical approach appears sound with minor methodological/reporting gaps that do not overturn the main conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a relevant recent baseline (interactive learning with weak denotational supervision, Wang et al. 2016) and evaluates on the same Voxelurn setting, reporting comparable metrics (percent naturalized utterances, number of structures built). It clearly analyzes how its definitional rule induction and alignment heuristics differ from prior methods and acknowledges limitations of selection-from-candidate supervision for large action spaces. However, only one explicit baseline is reported and some experimental parity details (e.g., hyperparameters, exact training conditions) are not mentioned, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(4): The paper compares against an appropriate recent baseline (interactive weak denotational supervision from Wang et al., 2016) on the same Voxelurn setting and reports relevant metrics (structures built, percent naturalized utterances). Differences between the proposed interactive-definition + alignment approach and prior methods are analyzed (e.g., infeasibility of selection-from-candidate supervision, need for alignment to generalize). However, the summary does not indicate whether prior methods ever perform comparably or better, and only a single primary baseline is reported, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a relevant recent baseline (interactive learning with weak denotational supervision, Wang et al. 2016) and evaluates on the same Voxelurn setting, reporting comparable metrics (percent naturalized utterances, number of structures built). However, only one explicit baseline is reported and some experimental parity details (e.g., hyperparameters, exact training conditions) are not mentioned, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—making programming-language-like interfaces more natural and scalable through interactive user definitions—and demonstrates this at nontrivial scale (240 complex voxel structures built; naturalized language comprised 74.7% of the last 10K utterances). Its claim that user-provided definitions and alignment-based chart transplantation let the system generalize beyond high-precision but undergeneralized rule induction addresses a practical bottleneck (selection-from-candidate supervision infeasible for large action spaces). These results are likely to change how researchers approach interactive semantic parsing and open new directions for user-driven language extension, with broader applicability to other complex action domains. | B(4): The paper addresses an important practical bottleneck—scaling natural language interfaces to large action spaces—by showing that interactive user definitions can effectively ‘‘naturalize’’ a core programming language for complex tasks (Voxelurn) where selection-from-candidate supervision is infeasible. Empirical evidence (users collaboratively built 240 complex voxel structures and naturalized utterances grew to 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) supports substantial user adoption, and the conservative alignment-based grammar induction that generalizes paraphrases while preserving precision is a pragmatic technique likely to influence follow-up work. These results open clear new directions for interactive grammar induction and user-driven language extension and are likely to be adopted or built upon by researchers developing practical semantic parsers and NL interfaces.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—making programming-language-like interfaces more natural and scalable through interactive user definitions—and demonstrates this at nontrivial scale (240 complex voxel structures built; naturalized language comprised 74.7% of the last 10K utterances). Its claim that user-provided definitions and alignment-based chart transplantation let the system generalize beyond high-precision but undergeneralized rule induction addresses a practical bottleneck (selection-from-candidate supervision infeasible for large action spaces). These results are likely to change how researchers approach interactive semantic parsing and open new directions for user-driven language extension, with broader applicability to other complex action domains.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an important practical bottleneck—scaling natural language interfaces to large action spaces—by showing that interactive user definitions can effectively ‘‘naturalize’’ a core programming language for complex tasks (Voxelurn) where selection-from-candidate supervision is infeasible. Empirical evidence (users collaboratively built 240 complex voxel structures and naturalized utterances grew to 74.7% of the last 10K utterances) supports substantial user adoption, and the conservative alignment-based grammar induction that generalizes paraphrases while preserving precision is a pragmatic technique likely to influence follow-up work. These results open clear new directions for interactive grammar induction and user-driven language extension and are likely to be adopted or built upon by researchers developing practical semantic parsers and NL interfaces.

main_claims: Seeding a system with a core programming language and allowing users to define naturalized constructs (head/body definitions) lets users incrementally build a richer, more convenient language interface for complex actions.
Interactive user-provided definitions scale language learning to complex action spaces (Voxelurn) that are infeasible to supervise by selecting from candidate parses alone.

Aligning head and body utterances to transplant derivations into the chart improves generalization (e.g., inferring paraphrases like 'up' -> 'top') while maintaining precision via conservative heuristics.
In a voxel world, a community of users taught one system and built 240 complex voxel structures; over three days use of the naturalized language rose to 74.7% of the last 10K utterances.
_summary.methodology: Seed a core programming language and learn interactively from user definitions (head utterance -> body of known utterances); induce grammar rules from these definitions and extend parsing charts via alignment heuristics to generalize paraphrases, implemented on SEMPRE and trained with online AdaGrad updates.
baselines: Interactive language learning with weak denotational supervision (Wang et al., 2016)
metrics: Number of complex voxel structures built (240)
Percentage of utterances using the naturalized language (74.7% of last 10K utterances)
limitations: Selection-from-candidate supervision is infeasible for large action spaces (e.g., >50 candidates).
Initial rule induction from definitions yields high-precision but undergeneralizes; alignment extension is needed.
Chart extension via alignment is only performed when the definition body consists of one utterance (paraphrases).
Alignment-based rule induction is applied conservatively because low-precision rules significantly degrade user experience (only when 1 or 2 tokens were left unmatched).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - natural language interfaces / semantic parsing
